extra.message: User-controlled request.path is interpolated via f-string into render_template_string(), enabling Server-Side Template Injection. An attacker can craft a URL containing Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., /{{config}}) to execute arbitrary code on the server.

User input from form.registration_code.data is concatenated directly into a SQL query via f-string interpolation inside text(). An attacker can inject SQL by supplying a malicious registration code, e.g., ' OR 1=1 -- to bypass validation.

User input from request.args.get('search') is interpolated via f-string into a SQL text() clause: text(f"text like '%{search_param}%'". An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through the search parameter.

pickle.loads() is called on a base64-decoded value from the user-controlled 'preferences' cookie. Since pickle deserialization can execute arbitrary code, an attacker can craft a malicious cookie to achieve remote code execution on the server.

urlopen() is called with a user-supplied URL from form.url.data without any validation or allowlisting. An attacker can supply internal URLs (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/) to access internal services, cloud metadata, or local files.

Note text is rendered with the |safe filter ({{ note.text | safe }}), which disables Jinja2 auto-escaping. Since note text comes from user input via CKEditor, an attacker can store malicious JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers.

render_table() is called with safe_columns=['text'], which marks the text column as HTML-safe and bypasses auto-escaping. An attacker can store malicious JavaScript in note text that executes when search results are rendered.

render_table() is called with safe_columns=['text'], which marks the text column as HTML-safe and bypasses auto-escaping. An attacker can store malicious JavaScript in note text that executes when personal notes are rendered.

The /account POST endpoint (update_account) lacks the @login_required decorator, allowing unauthenticated users to submit account update requests. The GET /account route has @login_required but the POST handler does not.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The /darkmode POST endpoint (toggle_darkmode) lacks the @login_required decorator, allowing unauthenticated users to toggle the dark mode preference and interact with the preferences cookie mechanism.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The /accounts/<user_id>/notes endpoint allows any authenticated user to view ALL notes (including private ones) of any other user by changing the user_id URL parameter. There is no check that user_id matches current_user.id, and the query does not filter on the private field.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The /notes/<note_id>/delete endpoint deletes any note by ID without verifying that the note belongs to the current user. Any authenticated user can delete any other user's notes by guessing or enumerating note IDs.

The update_account function updates current_user.__dict__ with all form fields except 'password', including 'is_admin'. A non-admin user can submit is_admin=True in the POST request to escalate privileges to admin, since the server does not check whether the user is authorized to modify the is_admin field.
The Flask secret key is hardcoded as 'super secret key'. This key is used to sign session cookies, and with a known key an attacker can forge session cookies to impersonate any user including admins.
A static registration code 'a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764' is hardcoded in the database seed file. Anyone with access to the source code can register an account without authorization.

The SQLAlchemy engine is created with echo=True, which logs all SQL queries including parameters to stdout. This can expose sensitive data such as user emails, hashed passwords, and registration codes in logs.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

extra.message: The 'preferences' cookie is set without the Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite flags. Without Secure, the cookie is sent over HTTP. Without HttpOnly, JavaScript can read the cookie. Without SameSite, the cookie is sent with cross-site requests. The cookie contains pickled data which is an RCE vector if tampered with.
The 'preferences' cookie is set in the after_request handler without the Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite flags. Same risk as the toggle_darkmode set_cookie call.
